title: Simulation of vibrational dynamics using qubits and qudits
abstract: We investigate the quantum computing of the vibrational dynamics of CO$_2$ and H$_2$O by constructing the vibrational Hamiltonian in qubit and qudit form by two types of qubit encodings (binary and direct) and a qudit encoding. We simulate the time-dependent vibrational population transfer using the three different encodings, including the effect of noise and find that the qudit encoding leads to the most accurate results both for CO$_2$ and H$_2$O because of the small number of terms in the qudit Hamiltonian as long as the same values of the entangling gate error rates are adopted.
summary: This paper investigates using qubits and qudits to simulate the vibrational dynamics of CO2 and H2O molecules. It finds that qudit encoding is superior for noisy quantum hardware because it requires fewer gates and is more resilient to decoherence. This makes qudits a promising approach for high-fidelity molecular spectroscopy on near-term quantum devices.
main_problem: Comparing the efficiency and accuracy of different quantum encoding schemes (binary qubit, direct qubit, and qudit) for simulating the vibrational dynamics of molecules under the influence of noise.
main_result: Qudit encoding provides the most accurate and efficient simulation because its Hamiltonian contains fewer terms, leading to reduced cumulative error from depolarizing noise and fewer required gates.
method: The study uses first-order Suzuki-Trotter decomposition for time evolution and a completely depolarizing noise model to evaluate the impact of gate errors on vibrational population transfer.
model_or_system: The physical models consist of the vibrational dynamics of CO2 (two-mode) and H2O (three-mode) molecules, using an anharmonic Hamiltonian that includes harmonic oscillator terms and third-order anharmonic coupling.
key_observables: Time-dependent vibrational populations and their Fourier transforms (vibrational spectra).
important_parameters: Maximum vibrational quantum number (v_max), gate error rates (10^-3 and 10^-5), and the number of two-qubit/qudit gates.
main_assumptions: The anharmonic potential is limited to third-order terms, and the noise is modeled strictly as a completely depolarizing channel.
figures_summary: Figures show the scaling of Hamiltonian terms and operator orders, time-dependent populations and Fourier spectra for H2O under different noise levels, and the decay time comparison between encodings.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the encoding schemes, defines the molecular Hamiltonians for CO2 and H2O, details the simulation and noise models, presents comparative results for gate counts and accuracy, and discusses error thresholds and experimental relevance.
why_interesting: It provides a practical comparison of qudit vs. qubit architectures for molecular spectroscopy, demonstrating how higher-dimensional encodings can mitigate noise in NISQ-era simulations of many-body molecular dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
